title: Metta: Developing Goodwill
subtitle: The Heart's Default Position
epigraph: Whatever you experience, if it's arising and passing away, how can it be you?
paragraphs: We have quite a bit to cover this evening. I want to touch on at least three areas. First, I'll revise—and for some of you this may be new—the Buddha's teaching on how we condition ourselves. Then I'll say something about metta and what we call the illimitables. And finally, I want to address forgiveness.

There are two key points about the Buddhist teaching on conditioning. The first is that all our conditioning is self-made. That's a bit difficult to grasp at first. All the information and experiences that come at you from the world are worked upon through an inner process—you react to them within yourself and create the conditionings you end up with.

So all those things we tend to suffer from—the depression, anxiety, all of that—and on the positive side, over-excitement, indulgence, all that part is actually self-created. You can go further and say the whole world is self-created if you include your body, because your body filters what comes in. If you're blind, you obviously don't live in a visual universe. And then of course the society you live in also filters things out through its own prejudices.

Everything that comes to us is worked upon within ourselves. Although people influence us and our society influences us—the very language influences us—the fact is that we end up creating our own worlds. Even here in this room: Theravada Buddhism isn't an idealism. It doesn't say this room doesn't exist, that when we all walk out it disappears. The room has its own reality, inverted commas. But it does say that the room we experience is peculiar to us.

Actually speaking, there are ten different rooms here, because each one of us has a different perspective on it, a different feeling about it. In that way you begin to understand that we're actually living in our own worlds. And they're parallel, sort of. They're not always harmonious.

When we realise we're living in our own world, the accent falls on us to do something about it. That gets rid of all blame. You can't blame. There comes a point of, shall we say, maturity where you realise you can't continue to blame your parents, your society, and all the rest of it for the state of mind you actually have to bear with.

Taking personal responsibility for oneself is the true beginning of the spiritual path, because that's beginning to take hold of your life. That's where you decide what changes you're going to make and how you're going to make them. The metta practice we're doing is part and parcel of that.

So where does this process begin? It begins with an intention. That's why, for those of you who've practised Vipassana, seeing your intentions is that crucial point where you have the space to notice whether this intention is wholesome or unwholesome, whether it's doing you any good or not. It's in that little space that you can say, no, I won't go down there—or yes, I will.

If for one moment you lose that moment of intention, it very quickly moves into identification. What was a wanting becomes I want. And at that point, really, it's virtually impossible to stop the action flowing out from the intention.

Every time you walk past Costa Coffee and Starbucks and the coffee grabs hold of your nose, there's want. If you're not quick enough, you're in there having a cup of coffee—whether you liked it or not, whether you wanted it or not, whether you needed it or not. It's those habitual actions we get caught up in that we have to come back on. And the way we do it is by having this very sharp mindfulness to see intentions as they arise.

The role of intention is crucial in our conditioning. The next step, which we've already touched upon, is the process of bringing intention from a potential into an actual. If I stand outside Costa Coffee thinking I'd like a cup of coffee, nothing's happening. That's the power of the old conditioning of wanting coffee. As long as I stay there with it, no matter how uncomfortable it may feel, nothing's happening—nothing in terms of it being developed.

But if we stay with that wanting and wait for it to die away, some energy within that conditioning is released. Some energy is destroyed because it hasn't been re-empowered. There is a moment when, having had the intention come up to have this coffee, an impulsion arises—a moment where energy goes into that intention and it manifests. That energy is your will.

The Buddha goes as far as to say that will is what he calls action. So what you do is not so important—it's how you do it. It ain't what you do, it's the way that you do it. The practice, then, is catching the intention and holding it so that you don't fall immediately into willing it, whereby you reinforce that old habitual pattern.

This is a crucial understanding in terms of the Buddha's psychology. For those of you who know the psychology of dependent origination, you'll recognise where he's putting his finger very clearly on the problem. It lies at that point of taṇhā—some of you will know that word—of desire.

These words from Scripture, translated from one language to another, are always difficult. Taṇhā is normally translated as craving, which seems to me always a little bit over the top, because sometimes you just want something and you just do it. You can't label that as craving. But what translators are trying to do is distinguish between a desire or intention which is wholesome and one which is unwholesome.

There is another word in the Buddha's language which is also translated as desire, but that one can be either wholesome or unwholesome. Whereas taṇhā, the word you get in dependent origination, is always unwholesome because it's always coming from the self.

Now, this self that we talk about, the teaching about not-self—you have to be careful here—it's not a denial, it's not a metaphysical proposition. The Buddha's not saying there is no self. It's a teaching tool. He's saying: whatever you experience, if it's arising and passing away, how can it be you? And it's that way of looking at things that begins to make you realise that all these things you've identified with are actually immaterial. They're not substantial. They don't amount to anything, any substance in the universe.

So we're in this flow of events, and it's most obvious in the body. This isn't the body we were born with. It may have a regular blueprint running through it, but it's definitely not the same body. And if we're to believe it, it's not the same body we had seven years ago. So next time you look in the mirror, look yourself in the eye and say: now, you weren't here seven years ago.

Just keep acknowledging the fact that the body itself is in a constant process of change, and yet we identify with it in very deep ways. So when death approaches, it's freak-out time. And that freaking out, that terrible shock that comes when it's our turn to go, is the measure of that attachment, the measure of that identity.

So taṇhā refers to those desires which come from wrong understanding, those desires which arise out of greed and hatred. Greed here is that whole business of acquisition, and hatred is that whole business of aversion. Actually, it's translated as aversion because we don't have a word which grasps the total meaning of the original Pali—but it also includes fear. When you come across something threatening, you either want to obliterate it, annihilate it—or if it's too big, you run for it. You've always got this fight-and-flight syndrome, and that's included in the word. So whenever you hear people talk about greed and aversion, remember that aversion includes the fear we have.

Understanding the role of intention in our psychology, and understanding that nothing actually happens until we empower it—until we actually do something, whether as a train of thoughts, a process of speech, or an action—has a conditioning effect. If we don't empower an intention, the energy with which it comes is simply allowed to dissipate. And that's basically how you get rid of habits and mental states you don't want. We learn this very clearly through vipassanā, but it's something you take out into daily life. It's not just something you do on the sitting stool.

Now here, of course, we have another thing we're doing, which is the good side of desire—and this comes from another centre. That's the important thing to understand. There is within us a different centre—call it Buddha, call it your wisdom centre, doesn't matter what you call it—but it's coming from a different place than the one which is self-seeking. And mettā is what you're developing from that centre.

It's important if you practise vipassanā always to do mettā as well. In the Zen tradition, they'll say that compassion arises naturally with wisdom—which is true, which is true—but it does help to have a practice which, as it were, just eggs that little movement on.

In the Buddhist teaching, we've got this Eightfold Path, and the first step is right understanding. It's in right understanding that vipassanā is placed—vipassanā is seeing things as they really are. And then there has to be an attitudinal change. It has to drop systemically, a movement from understanding into the heart as an attitude. That's where it begins to manifest outward through your thought, your speech, and your actions. And those are your next steps: right speech, right action, right livelihood.

If the process stops anywhere along that path, the spiritual life becomes stilted—twisted, even. People who practise vipassanā without mettā face a real danger: the aloofness you must have in vipassanā, that equanimity you find from the observation post within yourself where you can feel and see and watch things—very quickly, shall we say, it can slip into indifference. And once it becomes indifference, it moves to callousness. Basically, you've lost the path.

So to keep this equanimity alive, there has to be another practice—one that re-engages that observer within you into the psycho-physical organism and then into the world. That's what mettā does. It helps you to re-engage.

Samatha is slightly different. Mettā is a samatha practice, but it's more pointed to the outflow, to engagement—these are the illimitables. Samatha itself is really more to do with finding calmness, peace within us, which again is a great help. It's definitely an advantage if you're going to do vipassanā to have that easy basis of entering into a very quiet, relaxed, focused state. But it's not necessary in itself to do vipassanā.

And again, samatha—just like this practice—can become very selfish. You can practise it to the point where you begin to really get blissed out. One of the things about doing that is you realise these internal blissful states aren't dependent on anything out there. Then you begin to understand why people can live in rags, live under trees, eat what they're given—because it doesn't matter anymore. Their bliss is within them.

The problem, of course, is that this isn't connected to the world. So when such a person enters the world and someone stands on their foot, an enormous amount of anger can arise. That's the danger of doing something like this without the purification process of vipassanā.

Now, living in ordinary daily life, living in the world, that kind of withdrawal really becomes impossible because you have to engage with people. You have to put this into practice. And the heart will grow quite naturally with that. To my mind, it actually grows much better because of that interconnectedness.

There are two types of meditation. Vipassanā is this investigation of ourselves. Samatha practice is to do with establishing, creating beautiful states of mind. I'm sure you've heard of people looking at candles, doing regulated breathing. All those are little tricks to get this very one-pointed singleness of attention. The mind becomes like a laser beam, and it creates this lovely energy within you.

Mantras are the same. Anything like that—coloured circles, gazing upon the beloved's face, all that sort of stuff. Anything which produces a beautiful state. But remember: if that beautiful state is dependent on something out there, then that something always has to be there for this to arise. If it's created from within ourselves, then of course it's always there when we want it. That's the difference between what you might call a worldly practice as opposed to a spiritual one.

So now, recognising mettā as a practice which re-engages us both with ourselves and through ourselves into the world, we have to be clear as to what this mettā actually is. We touched upon it yesterday: it's an attitude. Attitudes pre-exist emotions. An attitude is the heart's way of relating to something, and then the heart begins to resonate—that's what we call an emotion, the emotional feeling part of the attitude. But the attitude is prior to that.

During your practice you will have been moving from that attitude of wishing people well while your heart isn't responding—it just feels tired or fed up. And then you realise: ah, this attitude is coming from somewhere behind or before the heart actually begins to resonate.

The big mistake is to confuse those lovely feelings with goodwill. Because then you think, I'm not practising goodwill unless I feel loving. And that's where we get confused—mixing up an emotion with the actual attitudinal state, which is simply being in a state of goodwill with people.

So you can see this is very different from love which arises from attachments. You have to be careful here. Attachment gets a bad name in Buddhism, but you have to make a distinction between something which is unskillful or unwholesome and something which is evil. Attachments aren't evil in the sense that murdering somebody is evil. But they are definitely unwholesome.

Consider a mother's attachment to her child—it's absolutely natural. I can't imagine a mother not having an attachment to her child. But what happens, of course, is that attachment puts a certain pressure on the child, because the attachment always wants the child to be as the mother would want them to be. And that's where the problem lies. That's why you get kicked back at teenage—because of that contradiction.

Now, to separate out the pure love that a mother has for her child from the attachment is very difficult. But it's there, and the wise person will slowly distinguish between the one and the other, knowing that one of them is actually turning the person into an object.

That's what attachment does. Every time you act out of attachment, that person is being used to please you. They become an object. They've lost their humanity. No matter how small the moment, you're dehumanising them.

The way you overcome attachment is always to be open to the other person's point of view, to their state. And that stepping into their consciousness, their way of looking at life—that is the act of love. That is the act of compassion.

When you see them, just ask yourself: what do you want? What do you want from them? There's your desire, coming from some place where they have to feed into your lack. This word 'lack' is often used as a translation for dukkha, which we usually render as unsatisfactoriness or suffering. But what 'lack' tells us is that there's an emptiness there, and we're seeking to fill it with things from the outside world. And of course they don't deliver.

The word 'attachment' in a sense describes a one-way process, whereas 'connection' goes both ways. And as I say, you connect by being open to the other, by just listening full-heartedly, open-heartedly, to what they have to say. That cuts through. Then of course you may feel a certain disappointment, a certain frustration, a certain fear when the person isn't going to feed into your emptiness. So that's the bad emptiness—that's not the emptiness of Mahayana, that's something else entirely.

You can see it's also not romance. Romance is very specific, isn't it? You can't fall in love with anybody and everybody. And that too is a form of attachment—the joy, the deliciousness of two personalities meeting at that point. It's very delicious. But as you know, three months down the line you often wonder what the hell you were doing.

Of course, it starts there, and if the relationship develops, it develops into a true love. And mettā is obviously not erotic love either—that's very flimsy and transient and empty. If you just practise making love for its own sake, it becomes an empty experience because the whole of the human being is not involved. Basically, it's a prostitution.
I always love Woody Allen's little comment on this. He says sexual pleasure by itself is an empty experience—but as empty experiences go...

So what is this mettā then? Well, just ask yourself: what would you want from a good friend? As those qualities come to you, that's what mettā is. Whatever companionship, open-heartedness, and so on—all the pleasant things you would want from a good and close friend—that's mettā. That's all it is. And mettā is actually connected to the word mitra, which means friend.

Now you can see that mettā is your basic relationship to all sentient beings. Last night we practised that awareness of just relaxing into the present moment, and I pointed that out as your default position. If we can keep coming back to that state of just being in the present moment with an empty mind—what do I mean by that? I mean no thought. When you hear in Zen 'no mind', they don't mean you've lost it. They just mean no thought.

If you can bring yourself every so often during the day—not just in that meditation, but every day—just to collapse, as it were, just draw yourself into the present moment, you do that. I find the easiest way is by putting my attention on hearing. Just by putting my attention on hearing, I immediately come into the present moment. And because I'm hearing and listening, I'm not responding. So it's a position of real equanimity. It's a position where I'm very open.

Here's another trick I've found: put your attention on the left side of your body—your right eye focused on the left side. This draws energy away from the speech centre of your brain and cuts out speech. It's quite magic, actually. You'll be surprised how it stops you talking.

If we consider this to be the default position of our whole lives—the ground out of which we rise, out of which we can see an intention, know it to be wholesome, and then move into action—then the default position for our relationship to sentient beings, specifically to human beings, is mettā.

From this basic position, the other two illimitables arise naturally. If you have a friend and they fall into bad times, you naturally want to help them. It's not a problem, is it? It just rises out of your friendship. And if your friend has good fortune and is happy, you're naturally happy for them. Sympathetic joy, reciprocal joy, all that delight in other people's happiness—it arises naturally. You don't have to work at it.

That's why the Buddha really pushes this mettā. I know that in later Buddhism they developed much more the idea of compassion, and one can understand why—the Buddha's teaching is about suffering and the end of suffering, so wanting to put an end to suffering is compassion. But in his original teachings, as they come out in the early scriptures, he's very much into mettā as a way.

The discourse on mettā really lays it out. He's asking us to constantly develop this attitude throughout the whole day, whenever we have a moment of time. And think of how much time we actually have: going from here to there, waiting for a bus, waiting at the lights, climbing stairs, waiting for a friend. If you add all those little bits together by the end of the day, I think they come out to a couple of hours, frankly.

What we usually do is just let the mind wander here and there—a complete waste of mental energy. It's during those times that you can bring these thoughts to mind, and it has that effect: constantly developing this heart of goodwill.

These are called illimitable because there's meant to be an innumerable number of beings. But more interestingly, it's a bit like numbers—you can always add ones. No matter how big your number is, you can always say 'and one'. It's the same with love, compassion, joy, equanimity. Their development is indefinite. You just keep on developing it, so long as you're awake. And in fact the teaching says exactly that: so long as you're awake, develop this attitude.

Now, we haven't practised the compassionate part, but basically it's very simple. You bring to mind people whom you know are suffering, and the phrase is: may you be free of suffering. It's as simple as that. And with joy: may you be joyful. May you continue to be joyful. May your joy increase.
At this stage, though, I would really suggest you keep it just as pure mettā, because, as I say, these other two things arise naturally.

Just as mettā has its near enemy—attachment—compassion has a near enemy too, and that's grief. We very quickly fall into grief for somebody, pity for somebody, instead of what compassion actually is. And again, that grief comes with attachment, because this person fulfils a role in your life. When they go, it leaves a gaping hole. Whether they fall into misfortune or whether they die, you're left with this sore within your heart. That's your grief.
But compassion is actually a joyful emotion. It fills the heart with joy because of the help you're giving people.

What I think compassion fills you with is a desire to do something—and that's when we get stuck with this business of impotence. We don't realise that we only have two rings around us: one of power, where we can do something; one of influence, where we can get others to do something about a situation; and finally a zone where we can't do anything at all. If you can't do anything, frustration arises, driven by grief and the wanting to act. But that's actually based on a wrong appraisal of ourselves. It's an ego thing.

It's only when you bear with your impotence and accept it fully that you realise the one thing you can still do is offer these goodwill practices towards them. That's how you overcome the frustration—because this is what you can do. And hopefully, it affects them.

You'll know that grief is a subtle enemy of compassion, especially when somebody dies and you grieve for them. Because you equate your grief with the amount of love you have for that person, you can't stop grieving. Every time your grief diminishes, you think you no longer love them. And you get yourself into this swirl down to a deep depression.

But when you realise that the grief you're feeling is actually the consequence of attachment, then love allows the person to go. Love is very liberating. These distinctions are sometimes difficult for us to grasp because we confuse compassion, love, and grief.

The obvious enemy of compassion, of course, is cruelty. That's why cruelty is the worst thing you can do in the Buddhist teaching. If you're cruel, the worst of hells is reserved for you—you go down to the Avīci hell where you really get thumped about. Because cruelty is the exact opposite of what the Buddha is teaching.

It's the same with joy. Sympathetic joy also has a subtle enemy, and that's excitement—where you're getting overly joyful, overly excited over something. To recognise that means you don't get the aftermath of indulgence.

This runs right the way through the ordinary joys of life. Remember that there are two things we have to do. First, we have to take the suffering out of pain. There's always going to be physical pain, and for as long as we're not fully liberated, there'll always be some form of emotional pain. But that's not suffering itself. Suffering comes because of our relationship to that—our not wanting to be there, our aversion to it, fear of it, and so on.

The other side of the coin is to take the indulgence out of pleasure, the indulgence out of the joys of life. If we don't do that, we end up with the aftermath. While you're enjoying something, fine—it's heaven, it's not a problem. It's afterwards. Afterwards, you get the craving for it again, the desire for it, the frustration when you can't get it, the grief when you lose it, and the underlying anxiety of losing something you're attached to.

This is underpinned by the fact that these practices are also called Brahmavihāra—the dwelling place of the highest gods. What we're saying by that is that they create the beautiful mind and the bountiful heart. That's how the gods live. Whether they exist or not is not a problem. That's the ideal, the idealism towards which these practices are taking us.
So that's the mettā side of things. And included in that, we have to practise forgiveness.

Going back to that original statement that all the suffering we experience is self-caused: the things that come out through harming others, harm done to us, and the harm we do to ourselves—all the suffering around that is also self-created. When we realise this, it's much easier to ask for forgiveness, much easier to forgive, and much easier to forgive oneself.

When we harm somebody, it creates within us a certain turbulence. The human heart cannot see another person's suffering without itself falling into some negativity. The worst outcome, of course, is callousness—where you're no longer connected to the suffering of another human being.

We've seen this going right back to the Nazis, right up to the present day. As soon as you dehumanise somebody, you have the wherewithal to create enormous suffering for them, physical suffering. And that callousness comes back on us—it turns our hearts into stone.

Once we open up to the fact that we've done harm to somebody, feelings of guilt and shame naturally arise. Guilt is the fear of consequences—we're not talking here about existential guilt, the sense that I am essentially evil. It's more like this: if you do that sort of thing, the comeuppance will come at some point. And shame is simply letting ourselves down; it's a loss of self-esteem.

When we recognise these as the consequences of doing something harmful, and we actually feel the guilt, get in contact with it, let it manifest—and we feel the shame, the embarrassment, let it manifest—then they actually become our guardians. The Buddha calls them our guardians of society. Because when we get close to an act we feel is unwholesome, these two come back to remind us of the consequences. In the old word, they're our conscience. And conscience is made up of the knowledge of what happens when you do something harmful.

So how do we overcome that burden of guilt and shame? Well, tomorrow we'll be practising it, and it works through the power of our own imagination, our own imaginative thinking. We set up these internal dialogues and bring ourselves round to asking for forgiveness. And then hopefully, if it's possible, we actually do it in real life.

Often, of course, we find that people we've harmed have disappeared years ago. People die and we've still got something on our hearts that rankles us. And then you make this insight: you don't need another person's forgiveness to be forgiven. That's a really important insight to have.
What you need to do is work with these inner states and cultivate that attitude of wanting to be forgiven, of asking for forgiveness. And that in itself is the forgiveness.

When the order meets—we meet every quarter, every half moon—when there's a group of four monks, four nuns, we confess. And most of the transgressions of our rule are forgiven on the confession. There's no tied-to-the-post-and-whipped confession. It's just having stated what you've done.

The response is: do you see what you've done? In other words, do you accept what you've done? And your response is probably, yes, I do. Then comes: do you undertake not to break that rule again? So there's a commitment to try and change your ways. Just the ability to state clearly that one has done wrong is in fact the process of self-forgiving, of being forgiven.

If you can put it right, if you've done something wrong which you can actually put right, all the better—then you stop the karmic flow. For instance, if you said a sharp word to somebody and you know they're glowering, using it against you behind your back, then just go up and say, look, I'm sorry for that. It immediately cuts the flow of that bad karma. Very simple to do.
But if you've got too much pride, if you feel you were righteous, that you should have been even more cruel—of course it just keeps going. There's no end to it.

When it comes to forgiving those who hurt us, we're on the receiving end. Just consider that point where somebody has said something which has annoyed you a bit, and how we keep repeating the insult over and over again until it builds up into this huge fire, and you have to take an aspirin. What you realise is that you're creating your own mental state. This is the point.
As soon as you realise that, you say, well, do I have to do this? Do I have to turn something like that into a huge drama? So it's again this ability to forgive.

And it helps to understand what forgiveness really means. Having forgiven somebody, you realise that they're suffering too—either from callousness, which leads to things like loneliness because nobody wants to know somebody who's sarcastic and hurtful, or they're suffering from their own feelings of guilt and shame. That's where your compassion comes in. You want to release them from their guilt and shame. So you offer them that opportunity by saying, look, let's forget that. Sorry it happened, but I forgive you.

We do that when we realise that in the act of forgiving, we completely empty the heart of all that rubbish we've burned up in there. And the more you do it, the easier it becomes—it just turns into a habit. Eventually there comes a point in our lives when even as the insult is given, forgiveness arises with it. So there's never any pain around that.

There's a lovely image the Buddha gives of somebody set upon by thieves and cut limb from limb with their machetes. He says that if at any point hatred or anger should arise, they're no disciple of mine. When I once said this in a group, somebody said, 'Well, the Buddha had a great sense of humour.' I said, he might have had, but I think it actually means what he says.

There is a way of receiving harm from somebody that doesn't bring that energy of anger from us. This is where we begin to understand, really, the psychology of non-violence. It's only when you realise that nobody can actually make you angry—they can hurt your body, sticks and stones may hurt my bones and all that—but nobody can actually cause you internal suffering.

And therefore you're ready to, in a way, absorb their anger. I'm sure you've experienced this in your life—somebody's come at you angrily and you've just said, 'Oh, I'm sorry.' And that 'I'm sorry,' just actually taking responsibility for that moment, drains the anger out. Whereas if you'd stood your ground and butted them, you'd have got into some sort of rage, a road rage or something.

Now you may not have been guilty; it might have been somebody else who did it. But you prove that afterwards. The important thing is to undermine the anger by receiving it—which is difficult. Our normal reaction is to defend ourselves.

This business of forgiving somebody—we get confused. We think we're trying to forget it, but trying to forget something is just another way of suppressing it. And we know that suppression doesn't do us any good. It just lies there as a turbulence within the mind, within the heart.

Sometimes we think we have to excuse them. But if somebody's robbed from us, they've robbed from us. Full stop. Not to excuse it—they've been robbers, they've done an evil thing. You don't have to lessen their act, but you can forgive them for it. And you can ask for compensation, for heaven's sake, if you feel it's right, without feeling that you're being vengeful.

Sometimes we get a feeling of letting them off, almost condoning what they did, because we get into this business of punishment. You often hear politicians saying they're putting people in prison for punishment and all that. But punishment doesn't hold any water in Buddhist understanding, because whenever you do something harmful, there's always some harmful result. So punishment is coming from some other centre.

It's coming from a centre where you're trying to frighten the person so they'll never do that again—a sort of deterrence. You cut your hand off if you steal. That works, is the point. But the punishment can't fit the crime. If it fits the crime, then you'll have another go. So if punishment is to be a deterrent, it's got to be something that really doesn't make the crime worth it.

And of course, the other thing about punishment is that it's a way of slaking our vengefulness. That doesn't work either. In the United States, in some states, you can watch the execution of the person who murdered your family member. And the case is that people come out unsatisfied. They wanted it to be more painful—because that's the problem with revenge, isn't it? You can't satisfy hatred. Hatred eats on hatred. It feeds itself on hatred.

So now, if you come to the understanding that you can't punish somebody, that there's no point in eating your own heart out because somebody's harmed you—even going back to childhood, remember—then that process of forgiving begins. And it helps to stand in the person's shoes.

We can do that more easily when we move into that third category: forgiving ourselves—for what we've done to ourselves through whatever it might be. Overeating, sex, drugs, rock and roll, all that sort of stuff.
Forgiving ourselves is probably the most difficult thing to do. It helps to understand that all our unwholesome actions have arisen because of a mistake. This is a crucial understanding.

The Buddha begins his account of our psychology with the word avijjā, usually translated as ignorance. But 'ignorance' gives you the impression that you're at fault—you should have known, you're just ignorant. That's not what the word means. It's neutral: you simply don't know. And out of that not knowing, there's been a mistake.

The mistake is who we think we really are. The mistake is to believe that we really are human beings, that we really are this body, this mind, this heart. Once we believe that, we have to seek happiness—which is what the heart wants, it wants peace and happiness—in the world. And how can you do that apart from accumulating things?

The self has to feel safe, so it feels safe by having big bank accounts, a very secure job, a very secure relationship. But of course, as soon as you accumulate something, you're in conflict with other people who want what you've got. Accumulation leads you immediately into a conflictual relationship with life. Then you find you have to guard what you have—and there's your fear and anxiety. You have to get rid of people who seem to be undermining your happiness, and so you enter into this whole battle that we call life.

Once you realise it's been a mistake, it takes away this feeling of being essentially evil. And here's the knowledge: just as this not knowing, through the practice of vipassanā, through spiritual practice, ends up at knowing—what we call wisdom—from the heart's point of view, it's begun at a point of innocence. The heart didn't know; it made a mistake, just like a child might light a match and burn a house down. It's a bit cruel to put a child in jail.

That innocence is corrupted because of this wrong understanding, and we end up with all this suffering. Then we get to a point where the suffering is so great we think, I'd better do some meditation. So we start meditating and we come back on the process, we devolve—but that process doesn't return us to an original not-knowing innocence. It returns us to a purity which matches the wisdom.

That's why in the spiritual life you can't grow in wisdom without growing in virtue. The two go together: one depends on the mind and the other depends on the heart. So when you find yourself balking against the precepts, that is a measure of our delusion, of our mistake.
Recognising that is the first step. The second is this: there's no need to punish ourselves. All we have to do is bear with the consequences of things we've done wrong. That's all.

I know somebody who, by a simple mistake—it wasn't even anything evil or wrong—just wanted to listen to music and put the instrument he was playing right next to his ear. Over time it damaged his ear and gave him terrible tinnitus. Then he had this struggle of hating it and fighting it. But by undermining that process, by just bearing with it and seeing it as a consequence of delusion, it became just a little background whistling. It doesn't impinge upon his life anymore.

It's the same with people who are suddenly disabled in some way. If the disability isn't accepted, if they're constantly fighting it, they just drive themselves into despair—and as you know, in some cases, suicide. But once it's accepted totally and becomes part and parcel of the way they live, it doesn't undermine the quality of life. Simple as that.

So that's the process we're going through. We're developing the heart of goodwill, developing the heart of compassion, and then through forgiveness we correct our relationship to the world where there's been harm—either done to us, or by us to others, or by us to ourselves.

Remember, you can't do all this in a weekend. This is a process you have to take with you into your daily life. And I think you'll find that the more you practise it, the more you get the benefits—tremendous, tremendous benefits.

And it's not as though you have to go at it all the time. It's the constant little bits that matter—in the morning, or during the day when you feel a bit upset about something, immediately going into the process of forgiveness. Just those small moments, practised persistently over time, change us completely. It's the persistence that's important: doing it whenever the occasion arises.
So I can only hope my words have been of some assistance. May you be liberated from all suffering—sooner rather than later.
